raw_text:
Jun
17
Press Release
Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
Read More

ai_summary.summary: Legislator Gaydos issued a statement about impact fee revenue being distributed to local communities in Pennsylvania. Impact fees are charges collected from natural gas drillers that are then allocated to municipalities and counties for local projects and priorities. The release highlights how these funds support local government needs.

ai_summary.summary: This is a listing of press releases from the Pennsylvania Department of Revenue, not a single legislator release. The releases cover routine administrative actions including monthly tax collection reports, the Property Tax/Rent Rebate Program application cycles, a new Working Pennsylvanians Tax Credit, and various taxpayer assistance initiatives spanning late 2024 through mid-2026. No single legislative action is being described.

title: Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
raw_text:
Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
June 17, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) today highlighted nearly $251,000 in Marcellus Shale impact fee revenue headed to municipalities across the 44th Legislative District, along with more than $2.04 million for Allegheny County. title: Gaydos Supports Measure to Reduce PFAS Exposure in Everyday Consumer Products
raw_text:
Gaydos Supports Measure to Reduce PFAS Exposure in Everyday Consumer Products
June 10, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) today announced her support for legislation that would prohibit the use of harmful per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) chemicals in a variety of high-contact consumer products commonly used by Pennsylvanians.
House Bill 2145
, which passed the House with bipartisan support, would ban the intentional use of PFAS in products such as cosmetics, dental floss, menstrual products and certain infant and children's items.
âI was pleased to co-sponsor this legislation and vote in favor of it because protecting the health and safety of Pennsylvania families should always be a priority,â said Gaydos. âMany of these products are used every day by families who may not realize they are being exposed to potentially harmful chemicals. This commonsense measure takes an important step toward reducing that exposure while giving manufacturers time to adapt.â
PFAS are a class of synthetic chemicals that have been linked to serious health concerns, including cancer, immune system suppression, fertility issues, pregnancy-related complications and other adverse health effects. Often referred to as âforever chemicals,â PFAS do not readily break down in the environment and can accumulate in soil, water and the human body over time.
Research has shown that PFAS-containing consumer products can contribute to environmental contamination, including groundwater and drinking water supplies, when discarded in landfills. Children may be especially vulnerable to exposure due to their frequent contact with products that can contain these chemicals.
The legislation would give manufacturers time to transition to safer alternatives while reducing the health and environmental risks associated with PFAS.
House Bill 2145 now advances to the Pennsylvania Senate for consideration.
Representative Valerie Gaydos
44th Legislative District

raw_text:
Gaydos Introduces Bill to Close Megan's Law Reporting Loophole, Strengthen Public Safety
July 1, 2026
HARRISBURG - To strengthen Pennsylvania's Megan's Law and improve community safety, Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) will be introducing legislation to close a loophole in the state's residency reporting requirements for registered sex offenders.
âMeganâs Law was created to help protect our communities by ensuring accurate information is available to law enforcement and the public,â said Gaydos. âCurrently, however, someone can avoid reporting an address simply by moving between locations before reaching 30 consecutive days, which creates a gap in the law that puts transparency and public safety at risk. My legislation would close that loophole and help ensure residency information more accurately reflects where registered offenders are spending significant time.â
Under current law, individuals required to register under Meganâs Law must report any address where they reside for 30 or more consecutive days. Gaydos said constituents recently brought to her attention a situation involving a registered offender who was spending a significant amount of time at an alternate location but was able to avoid reporting that address because the individual did not remain there for 30 consecutive days.
As a result, neighbors and local law enforcement may not have an accurate picture of where a registered offender is actually living or spending significant time, limiting the effectiveness of Meganâs Law and undermining its intent to promote public safety through transparency.
âMy legislation would remove the word âconsecutiveâ from the residency reporting requirement,â said Gaydos. âUnder the change, individuals subject to Megan's Law would be required to report any address where they reside for a total of 30 or more days within a calendar year, regardless of whether those days occur consecutively.â
The legislation is intended to strengthen the effectiveness of Meganâs Law by ensuring residency reporting requirements better reflect where registered offenders are living or spending substantial time, providing more accurate information for law enforcement and the communities they serve.

State Budget Reflects Responsible Investments Today, Says Gaydos
raw_text:
State Budget Reflects Responsible Investments Today, Says Gaydos
July 12, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) issued the following statement after voting in favor of the 2026-27 General Appropriations bill:
âPassing a state budget is about finding common ground and making responsible decisions for the people we represent. While not everyone will agree with every part of this budget, real leadership means working together to move Pennsylvania forward.
âThis $50.8 billion budget demonstrates greater restraint while investing in the priorities that matter most today. It is balanced without raising broad based taxes or tapping the Commonwealth's Rainy Day Fund, thanks in part to stronger than expected revenues, Medicaid payment cycle adjustments and responsible management of special funds. At a time when families are facing higher costs for groceries, housing and energy, it is important that we make responsible investments while protecting taxpayers and preserving affordability.
âThe budget delivers funding for our local schools, roads and bridges, career and technical education, and public safety. The additional $10 million investment in career and technical education will help prepare students for rewarding careers in high demand fields, while funding for the Rapid Bridge and Road Deployment Program will improve critical infrastructure across the Commonwealth.
As Pennsylvania prepares for the jobs and industries of the future, we must make sure economic growth is balanced with affordability. We should not have to shoulder higher energy costs because of growing demand from large scale data centers or other energy intensive projects. As we invest in workforce development and infrastructure, we must ensure families, small businesses and employers remain our top priority.
âI also supported long overdue cost of living adjustments for pre Act 9 retirees and retired municipal police officers and firefighters who have not received an increase in more than 20 years. These dedicated public servants deserve to be recognized for their years of service.
âMy constituents expect us to spend their tax dollars responsibly. This budget invests in education, workforce development, infrastructure and public safety while protecting taxpayers, preserving our financial stability and supporting a stronger, more affordable Pennsylvania. It is my job to do what is best for the communities I represent, and I believe this budget accomplishes that. That is why I voted âyes.ââ
Representative Valerie Gaydos
44th Legislative District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives

title: Gaydos Announces $4 Million in State Funding for Economic Development, Public Safety Projects
raw_text:
Gaydos Announces $4 Million in State Funding for Economic Development, Public Safety Projects
July 17, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) today announced $4 million in state funding through the Redevelopment Assistance Capital Program (RACP) for two projects in the 44th Legislative District that will improve fire protection infrastructure and support advanced manufacturing growth.
âThese investments are about protecting lives, creating good-paying jobs and strengthening our local economy,â said Gaydos. âBy improving fire protection in Leetsdale and supporting advanced manufacturing in North Fayette Township, we are making our communities safer, more competitive and better positioned for long-term growth.â
The funding will be distributed as follows:
âĒ Leetsdale Park Early Suppression Fast Response (ESFR) Fire Main Extension, Leetsdale Borough: $1 million to install a new fire pump, fire mains throughout the industrial park, and upgraded service connections to buildings, enhancing fire protection capabilities for businesses and facilities within the park.
âĒ Mainspring Energy, North Fayette Township: $3 million to support the establishment of a new manufacturing facility capable of producing up to 1,000 linear generators annually. The expansion will help meet growing national demand for flexible, resilient energy technology while creating jobs and strengthening the regionâs advanced manufacturing economy.
The Redevelopment Assistance Capital Program is a Commonwealth grant initiative administered by the Office of the Budget that supports projects with regional economic, civic, recreational and infrastructure impact.
